name: Anthurium forgetii 'Variegated'
origin: Species native to Colombia; variegated collector form in cultivation
aboutText: Anthurium forgetii 'Variegated' is a stunning, compact understory aroid native to Colombia. Highly prized in collector circles, it is famous for its distinctive, nearly round, sinusless leaves that feature a plush velvety texture and contrasting pale venation. The rare variegated form is adorned with breathtaking cream-to-yellow marbling and sectoral variegation splashed across its deep green leaf blades. In cultivation, it performs best in warm, highly humid conditions with a very airy, well-draining substrate and bright indirect light.

morphology.variegation: Cream to yellow marbling and sectoral splashes

propagation.notes: Variegation in this form is unstable — cuttings may revert to the standard green form. Select cuttings from the most highly variegated growth, but results are not guaranteed.

careGuide.substrate: Very chunky, well-aerated mix: 40% orchid bark, 30% perlite, 20% sphagnum moss, 10% activated charcoal. Anthuriums suffocate in dense soil — roots need airflow.
careGuide.watering: Water when the substrate is nearly dry throughout. Less is more — overwatering is the primary killer of velvet anthuriums. Always use room-temperature water.
careGuide.humidity: 70–85% is essential. Below 60% causes stunted growth and curling leaves. A dedicated humidifier is strongly recommended for UK growers.
careGuide.fertilising: Low-nitrogen fertiliser (e.g. 5-10-10 or orchid fertiliser) at quarter strength every 3–4 weeks. High nitrogen produces lush but weak growth susceptible to pests.
careGuide.repotting: Reluctantly — only when completely root-bound (every 2–3 years). These plants dislike disturbance and may sulk after repotting.

commonProblems.fix: Remove from pot, trim affected roots, repot into fresh dry substrate and reduce watering
Increase humidity above 70% and move away from cold windows

commonProblems.cause: Common on velvet-leaf anthuriums; hard to detect early
commonProblems.fix: Inspect new growth and leaf undersides regularly; treat with neem oil or systemic insecticide at first sign

items: Inspect the newest leaf — variegation that is only present on older leaves and absent from new growth suggests the plant is reverting
Thrips are the primary pest concern: check undersides of all leaves, especially unfurling new growth, for fine silvery streaks or dark specks
Variegated anthuriums are high-value — insist on photos of roots, newest leaf (both sides), and full plant before any purchase over £100
Be aware that unstable chimeral variegation means future leaves may express differently — buy based on the plant's current state, not assumed future patterning

fieldNotes.title: The Circular Jewel of Colombia
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Anthurium forgetii is instantly recognizable because it completely lacks the upper sinus (the lobes at the top of the leaf are fused, making it almost circular). The green velvet texture is outstanding, but the variegated cultivar adds a new dimension with splashes of yellow and cream. A 2019 genetic study on Colombian understory aroids highlighted the species' unique leaf geometry as a highly specialized design to reduce water accumulation on the leaf blade, preventing fungal growth. In cultivation, it is slow and demands extremely high humidity.
